Unsafe Schools: The Global Water & Sanitation Crisis

A UNICEF Data Story on Children’s Right to Clean Water in Schools

Author

Sreeja Ajith

Published

January 4, 2026

The Crisis Hiding in Plain Sight

Every morning, millions of children walk into schools that lack basic clean water. They study, play, and grow in environments where handwashing is impossible, toilets are unusable, and dehydration is a daily reality. This is not a distant problem – it is happening right now, in over 140 countries tracked by UNICEF.

Indicators Used

This report focuses on two key UNICEF indicators:

  • Schools with limited drinking water services (%) – representing the proportion of schools where children lack reliable access to safe drinking water.
  • Population with inadequate sanitation (%) – capturing broader environmental conditions that affect children’s health both inside and outside school.

Together, these indicators provide a comprehensive view of the water and sanitation challenges affecting children globally.

“Water and sanitation are not luxuries. They are human rights – and for children, they are the foundation of health, dignity, and education.” – UNICEF

Data Transformation

To ensure accurate and meaningful analysis:

  • Only Total population values were used to avoid gender-based duplication or bias
  • Country-level averages were calculated to summarise long-term trends across years
  • Missing values were removed to improve data reliability
  • External socioeconomic data (GDP, population, life expectancy) were merged to explore relationships between economic development and school conditions

Where Are Schools Failing Children Most?

The map below shows the average proportion of schools with limited drinking water access across all available years. Darker shades indicate a higher percentage of schools where children lack adequate water.

Show code 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(14, 7))
world_map_df.plot(ax=ax, color="#e0e0e0", edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3)
has_data = world_map_df.dropna(subset=["pct_limited_water"])
has_data.plot(
    column="pct_limited_water", ax=ax, cmap="Blues",
    edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3, legend=True,
    legend_kwds={"label": "% Schools with Limited Water",
                 "orientation": "horizontal", "shrink": 0.5,
                 "pad": 0.02, "aspect": 30}
)
ax.set_title("Schools Without Safe Water: A Global Snapshot",
             fontsize=15, fontweight="bold", color=UNICEF_DARK, pad=12)
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("chart_map.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.close()
display(IPImage("chart_map.png"))

Key observations from the map:

  • South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa show the highest rates of schools with limited water.
  • Latin America also shows pockets of concern, particularly in Central America.
  • High-income countries in North America, Western Europe, and Australia report near-zero rates.

The 15 Worst-Affected Countries

The bar chart below ranks the 15 countries with the highest average proportion of schools lacking adequate drinking water.

Show code 
p1 = (
    ggplot(top15, aes(x="country", y="pct_limited_water", fill="pct_limited_water"))
    + geom_col(width=0.75, show_legend=False)
    + geom_text(aes(label="pct_limited_water"),
                format_string="{:.1f}%", ha="left", size=8,
                nudge_y=0.5, color="#333333")
    + scale_fill_gradient(low=UNICEF_BLUE, high=UNICEF_DARK)
    + scale_y_continuous(expand=(0, 0), limits=(0, 78))
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(title="The 15 Countries Where Schools Lack Safe Water Most",
           subtitle="Average % of schools with limited drinking water | 2000-2023",
           x=None, y="Average % of Schools with Limited Water Access",
           caption="Source: UNICEF | unicef_indicator_1.csv")
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(plot_title=element_text(size=14, face="bold", color=UNICEF_DARK),
            plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
            plot_caption=element_text(size=8, color="#888888"),
            axis_text_y=element_text(size=10),
            axis_text_x=element_text(size=9),
            panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
            figure_size=(10, 7))
)
p1.save("chart_bar.png", dpi=150, verbose=False)
display(IPImage("chart_bar.png"))

What the data tells us:

  • Libya stands out with a staggering 67% average – meaning two thirds of schools have limited water access.
  • Panama, Syria, Nepal, and Solomon Islands all exceed 35%, reflecting both conflict and geographic isolation as key drivers.
  • The presence of middle-income countries like Mexico and India underlines that the crisis is also a governance and infrastructure failure.

Wealth vs. Water: Is GDP the Answer?

If clean water in schools were simply a matter of money, we would expect a perfect negative relationship between GDP per capita and school water deprivation. The scatterplot below tests this assumption.

Show code 
p2 = (
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x="gdp", y="pct_limited_water", size="population"))
    + geom_point(color=UNICEF_BLUE, alpha=0.6, show_legend=False)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color=UNICEF_RED, se=True, size=1.2)
    + scale_x_log10(labels=lambda l: [f"${x:,.0f}" for x in l])
    + scale_size_continuous(range=(1, 10), guide=None)
    + labs(title="Richer Countries Have Safer Schools -- But Not Always",
           subtitle="GDP per capita (log scale, 2015 USD) vs. % of schools with limited water | ~2020",
           x="GDP per Capita (log scale, constant 2015 USD)",
           y="% Schools with Limited Water Access",
           caption="Source: UNICEF & World Bank")
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(plot_title=element_text(size=14, face="bold", color=UNICEF_DARK),
            plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
            plot_caption=element_text(size=8, color="#888888"),
            figure_size=(10, 6))
)
p2.save("chart_scatter.png", dpi=150, verbose=False)
display(IPImage("chart_scatter.png"))

Interpreting the trend:

  • The negative regression line confirms that wealthier nations generally have better water access in schools – but the relationship is not perfect.
  • Several countries with moderate GDPs still show high deprivation rates, indicating that political will, conflict, and institutional capacity matter as much as raw wealth.

Are Things Getting Better? A Time-Series View

Progress is possible – but is it happening fast enough? The chart below tracks changes over time for the 5 most-affected countries.

Show code 
p3 = (
    ggplot(ts_df, aes(x="time_period", y="obs_value", color="country", group="country"))
    + geom_line(size=1.1)
    + geom_point(size=2.5)
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=[UNICEF_BLUE, UNICEF_DARK, UNICEF_ORANGE, UNICEF_GREEN, UNICEF_RED],
        name="Country")
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=range(2000, 2024, 2))
    + labs(title="Progress -- or Stagnation? School Water Trends Over Two Decades",
           subtitle="% of schools with limited drinking water | Top 5 most-affected countries | 2000-2023",
           x="Year", y="% Schools with Limited Water Access",
           caption="Source: UNICEF | unicef_indicator_1.csv")
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(plot_title=element_text(size=14, face="bold", color=UNICEF_DARK),
            plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#555555"),
            plot_caption=element_text(size=8, color="#888888"),
            axis_text_x=element_text(angle=45, ha="right", size=9),
            legend_title=element_text(size=10),
            figure_size=(11, 6))
)
p3.save("chart_timeseries.png", dpi=150, verbose=False)
display(IPImage("chart_timeseries.png"))

What the trends reveal:

  • Syria’s sharp upward spike reflects the devastating impact of civil war on educational infrastructure.
  • Nepal shows a concerning increasing trend, suggesting rapid urbanisation is outpacing infrastructure investment.
  • Panama and Solomon Islands display relatively flat trends – meaning the crisis is not self-correcting without targeted intervention.

What Must Change

The data is clear. The water and sanitation crisis in schools is not random – it follows patterns of poverty, conflict, and governance failure. But it is also not inevitable.

UNICEF calls for urgent action on three fronts:

  1. Targeted investment – Governments and donors must prioritise school WASH funding in the 15 highest-burden countries identified in this report.
  2. Conflict-sensitive programming – Countries like Syria demonstrate that water infrastructure must be protected and rapidly restored in conflict and post-conflict settings.
  3. Accountability mechanisms – Annual school-level reporting on water and sanitation access must become standard.

Every child deserves to learn in a school where they can wash their hands, drink clean water, and use a safe toilet. Until that is true everywhere, the work is not done.

Report prepared using UNICEF global indicator data. Visualisations created with plotnine in Python, rendered via Quarto.
